agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics platform introducing automated route optimization for drivers at shift start, with dynamic recalculation triggers, customer notification windows, vehicle-class enforcement, and configurable stop duration. This spans multiple bounded contexts: route planning, fleet/driver management, delivery lifecycle, and customer notifications.",
    "bounded_context": "Multiple contexts identified: (1) Route Planning — owns route calculation, optimization, recalculation triggers, stop time estimates; (2) Fleet Management — owns driver profiles, vehicle classes, licensing, zone assignments, shift lifecycle; (3) Delivery Management — owns delivery lifecycle (pending, in-transit, failed, completed), zone classification, package attributes (size/weight/class); (4) Customer Notification — owns notification triggers, delivery proximity windows, customer communication preferences. The feature as described crosses all four contexts, which is a design risk.",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shift", "definition": "A bounded work period for a driver, beginning when they clock in and ending when they clock out or complete all assigned deliveries. A shift is the lifecycle anchor for route assignment." },
      { "term": "Zone", "definition": "A geographic operational area to which drivers and deliveries are assigned. Zones are the unit of route scoping — a driver's route only includes deliveries within their assigned zone." },
      { "term": "Optimized Route", "definition": "An ordered sequence of delivery stops calculated by the system to minimize total travel time/distance for a driver within their zone, respecting all constraints (vehicle class, delivery windows)." },
      { "term": "Route Recalculation", "definition": "The act of re-optimizing a driver's remaining route in response to a defined trigger event (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation)." },
      { "term": "Pending Delivery", "definition": "A delivery that has been created and assigned to a zone but not yet assigned to a driver's route or currently in-transit." },
      { "term": "Failed Delivery", "definition": "A delivery attempt that was unsuccessful (e.g., recipient unavailable, access denied). Triggers route recalculation because the stop is removed or deprioritized." },
      { "term": "Manual Deviation", "definition": "When a driver physically departs from the system-suggested route sequence, detected via GPS or driver-reported action." },
      { "term": "Next 3 Stops Window", "definition": "A proximity notification threshold — the set of the next 3 upcoming stops on a driver's active route. When a customer's delivery enters this window, they receive a notification." },
      { "term": "Licensed Vehicle Class", "definition": "A classification of the vehicle a driver operates (e.g., motorcycle, van, truck, freight). Determines which deliveries are physically and legally eligible for assignment." },
      { "term": "Stop Duration", "definition": "The estimated time a driver spends at each delivery stop. Defaults to 4 minutes system-wide but is overridable per zone by the operations team." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A driver MUST be assigned an optimized route automatically when they start their shift, based on all pending deliveries in their assigned zone at that moment.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a business process trigger, not a data integrity constraint. The timing of route generation (shift start) is a policy decision — ops could decide to pre-generate routes or allow manual assignment instead.",
      "testable_assertion": "When a driver's shift status transitions to 'active', a route containing all currently pending deliveries in the driver's zone is generated and assigned within the system's SLA."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "A driver's route MUST be recalculated when a new delivery is added to their active zone during their shift.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Recalculation policy is a business decision. A VP could decide new deliveries queue for the next shift or are manually dispatched instead of triggering automatic recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with an active route, when a new pending delivery is created in the driver's zone, then the driver's route is recalculated to include the new delivery within the defined recalculation SLA."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "A driver's route MUST be recalculated when any delivery on their route is marked as failed.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a process policy. The business could choose to simply skip the stop without full recalculation.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with an active route containing delivery D, when D is marked as failed, then the route is recalculated excluding D (or repositioning D per retry policy) within the defined recalculation SLA."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A driver's route MUST be recalculated when the driver manually deviates from the suggested route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Deviation response is a business policy. The system could alternatively just flag the deviation without recalculating.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver with an active route, when the system detects the driver has deviated from the next suggested stop (e.g., skipped a stop or traveled to a non-next location), then the route is recalculated from the driver's current position."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Customers MUST be notified when their delivery enters the 'next 3 stops' window on a driver's active route.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Notification threshold (3 stops) is a business policy that could be adjusted. The notification itself is a customer experience decision, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer with a pending delivery on a driver's route, when that delivery becomes one of the next 3 stops (positions 1, 2, or 3 in the remaining route), then the customer receives exactly one proximity notification."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned any delivery that requires a vehicle class exceeding their licensed vehicle class.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a physical and legal constraint. A motorcycle physically cannot carry freight. Assigning such a delivery would be illegal (licensing violation), unsafe, and physically impossible. Violating this corrupts the system's operational integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every delivery assigned to a driver's route, the delivery's required vehicle class MUST be equal to or below the driver's licensed vehicle class. Any attempt to assign a mismatched delivery MUST be rejected by the system."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The default estimated stop duration is 4 minutes per delivery stop.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a configurable parameter, explicitly stated as overridable by ops. Pure business policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "When no zone-level override exists, route calculations use 4 minutes as the stop duration for each delivery."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The operations team can override the estimated stop duration on a per-zone basis.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an administrative policy capability, not a system invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "When an operations team member sets a stop duration override for a zone, all subsequent route calculations for that zone use the overridden value instead of the 4-minute default."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "A driver MUST NOT be assigned deliveries outside their assigned zone.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Zone assignment is the fundamental scoping mechanism for route optimization. Cross-zone assignment would break operational accountability, route coherence, and zone-based SLAs. If a delivery is in Zone A and a driver is in Zone B, assigning it violates the operational model.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every delivery on a driver's route must belong to the same zone the driver is assigned to for their current shift."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "Route recalculation MUST preserve already-completed stops and only re-optimize remaining stops.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Rewriting history of completed deliveries would corrupt delivery records, timestamps, and proof-of-delivery data. The system's state integrity depends on completed stops being immutable.",
      "testable_assertion": "After any route recalculation, all stops marked as 'completed' retain their original position, timestamp, and status. Only stops with status 'pending' or 'in-transit' are subject to reordering."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift and there are 5 pending deliveries in their assigned zone, all compatible with their vehicle class",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "The system generates an optimized route containing exactly those 5 deliveries and assigns it to the driver"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with 8 remaining stops",
      "when": "A new delivery is added to the driver's zone that is compatible with their vehicle class",
      "then": "The route is recalculated to include the new delivery, optimizing the sequence of all remaining (uncompleted) stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver has an active route and delivery at position 5 is marked as failed",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The route is recalculated excluding the failed delivery from the remaining stops, and the remaining sequence is re-optimized"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver has an active route with next stop A, but GPS shows they are heading to or arrived at stop C (position 3)",
      "when": "The system detects the deviation",
      "then": "The route is recalculated from the driver's current position, re-optimizing all remaining unvisited stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A customer's delivery is at position 5 on a driver's route",
      "when": "The driver completes the delivery at position 1 (causing positions to shift so the customer's delivery moves to position 4, then to position 3)",
      "then": "The customer receives a proximity notification exactly once when their delivery first enters the next-3-stops window"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A motorcycle driver is active in a zone containing both small-parcel and freight deliveries",
      "when": "The route is generated or recalculated",
      "then": "Only small-parcel deliveries compatible with motorcycle class are included; freight deliveries are excluded and the system does not error — they remain unassigned"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The operations team has set the stop duration for Zone X to 6 minutes",
      "when": "A route is calculated for a driver in Zone X",
      "then": "The route's time estimates use 6 minutes per stop, not the default 4 minutes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A driver has completed 3 of 8 stops on their route",
      "when": "A recalculation is triggered",
      "then": "The 3 completed stops remain unchanged in the route history, and only the 5 remaining stops are re-optimized"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A driver starts their shift but there are zero pending deliveries in their zone",
      "when": "The shift is activated",
      "then": "The driver is assigned an empty route and is placed in an 'awaiting deliveries' state; when a delivery enters their zone, a route is generated"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A customer's delivery is already in the next-3-stops window and a route recalculation occurs that keeps it in the window",
      "when": "The recalculation completes",
      "then": "The customer does NOT receive a duplicate notification"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Multiple recalculation triggers fire simultaneously (e.g., a new delivery added AND a delivery fails at the same time)", "expected_behavior": "The system must serialize or merge recalculation events to avoid race conditions. The resulting route must reflect both events. Two concurrent recalculations must not produce a corrupt or stale route." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is added to a zone where the only active driver has an incompatible vehicle class", "expected_behavior": "The delivery remains unassigned/pending. It must NOT be forced onto the incompatible driver. The system should surface this to dispatch/operations as an unassignable delivery." },
    { "scenario": "A driver's route is recalculated and a customer's delivery moves OUT of the next-3-stops window", "expected_behavior": "The customer who was already notified is NOT sent a retraction. If the delivery later re-enters the window, a second notification must NOT be sent (idempotency per delivery per shift)." },
    { "scenario": "A driver deviates but returns to the original route before the system detects it", "expected_behavior": "No recalculation is triggered. Deviation detection must have a threshold (time or distance) to avoid spurious recalculations from GPS jitter or brief detours." },
    { "scenario": "The operations team changes the stop duration for a zone while drivers are mid-shift", "expected_behavior": "Existing active routes should use the updated duration on the next recalculation, but should NOT trigger an immediate recalculation solely for a duration change. ETA estimates shown to customers should update progressively." },
    { "scenario": "All deliveries on a driver's route are completed or failed", "expected_behavior": "The driver's route transitions to 'completed'. The driver becomes eligible for new deliveries in their zone or their shift can end." },
    { "scenario": "A failed delivery has a retry policy (e.g., retry in 30 minutes)", "expected_behavior": "The spec does not define retry behavior. The failed delivery should be removed from the current route. Retry scheduling is a separate concern — if retried, it re-enters as a new pending delivery and triggers recalculation per BR-2." },
    { "scenario": "Two drivers are active in the same zone simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "The spec does not address multi-driver zone assignment. Deliveries must be partitioned between drivers — the same delivery must NOT appear on two routes simultaneously. This requires a delivery-to-driver assignment lock." },
    { "scenario": "Driver's shift ends while deliveries remain on their route", "expected_behavior": "Undelivered stops must be returned to 'pending' status and become eligible for reassignment. They must not be lost or stuck in an assigned-but-unserviced state." },
    { "scenario": "Notification sent for 'next 3 stops' but the driver completes all 3 stops in under a minute (batch drop-off at same building)", "expected_behavior": "The notification is still valid even if the delivery arrives sooner than expected. The notification is a 'heads up', not an ETA guarantee." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Route optimization directly reduces fuel costs, driver hours, and failed delivery rates. Proper vehicle-class enforcement prevents costly failed delivery attempts and potential regulatory fines. Configurable stop durations allow zone-specific tuning that improves route accuracy and on-time delivery rates, which drive customer retention revenue.",
    "operational": "Dynamic recalculation introduces computational load that scales with zone density and trigger frequency. Operations teams gain real-time control via stop duration overrides. Multi-driver zone scenarios and concurrent recalculation events require robust conflict resolution. Deviation detection requires reliable GPS infrastructure and threshold tuning to avoid false positives.",
    "user": "Customers receive proactive delivery proximity notifications improving satisfaction and reducing 'where is my package' support calls. Drivers receive optimized routes reducing cognitive load and decision fatigue. However, frequent recalculations mid-shift could frustrate drivers if the route keeps changing — this needs UX consideration.",
    "risk": "Vehicle class enforcement failure is the highest risk — regulatory, safety, and reputational. Race conditions in concurrent recalculation could produce stale or conflicting routes. Notification duplication would erode customer trust. Multi-driver zone scenarios without delivery locking could cause double-delivery attempts. The spec is silent on several critical operational scenarios (multi-driver zones, shift end handling, retry policy).",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers", "impact": "Receive auto-optimized routes reducing decision burden; but frequent mid-shift recalculations may cause confusion. Must be protected from illegal vehicle-class assignments." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Receive proximity notifications improving experience. Risk of duplicate or misleading notifications if not handled correctly." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Gains per-zone stop duration control. Must handle unassignable deliveries (vehicle class mismatch). Needs visibility into multi-driver zone partitioning." },
      { "group": "Dispatch/Planning", "impact": "Shift from manual route planning to automated system. Must trust the optimization algorithm. Need override capabilities for edge cases." },
      { "group": "Compliance/Legal", "impact": "Vehicle class enforcement is a regulatory requirement. System must produce audit trails for delivery-to-driver assignments." },
      { "group": "Finance", "impact": "Fuel and labor cost reduction from optimized routes. ROI depends on recalculation accuracy and adoption rate." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec clearly defines the core happy path but is silent on several critical operational scenarios that MUST be resolved before implementation: multi-driver zone partitioning, shift-end handling, failed delivery retry policy, deviation detection thresholds, and delivery locking. These are not edge cases — they are fundamental to a working system. The vehicle class constraint is clear and invariant, and the recalculation triggers are well-defined, but the absence of multi-driver semantics is a significant gap that could lead to double-assignments or unassigned deliveries.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Vehicle class enforcement is a hard invariant (BR-6)",
      "Three recalculation triggers are clearly defined (new delivery, failure, deviation)",
      "Customer notification window is well-specified (next 3 stops)",
      "Stop duration default and override mechanism is clear",
      "Zone scoping for route generation is unambiguous"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Multi-driver zone behavior — how are deliveries partitioned between concurrent drivers in the same zone?",
      "Deviation detection mechanism — what constitutes a 'deviation'? GPS threshold? Driver-reported? Time-based?",
      "Failed delivery retry semantics — does a failed delivery re-enter the queue or require manual intervention?",
      "Shift lifecycle — what happens to undelivered stops when a shift ends?",
      "Recalculation concurrency — how are simultaneous triggers serialized?",
      "Vehicle class hierarchy — is it a strict hierarchy (truck > van > motorcycle) or a multi-dimensional classification?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "One driver per zone is the primary operating model (spec does not address multi-driver zones)",
      "Vehicle class is a linear hierarchy where higher classes can handle lower-class deliveries",
      "Deviation detection is GPS-based with some distance/time threshold",
      "Failed deliveries are removed from the route and do not automatically retry",
      "Route optimization means minimizing total travel time/distance (spec does not define the optimization objective)",
      "The 'next 3 stops' notification is sent once per customer per delivery per shift (idempotent)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not address the scenario of multiple drivers active in the same zone simultaneously. This is not an edge case — it is fundamental to delivery assignment. Without a partitioning strategy, the system could double-assign deliveries or leave deliveries orphaned.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Customers"],
      "options": [
        "A: One driver per zone at a time (simplest, but limits throughput)",
        "B: Deliveries are exclusively assigned to one driver at route generation, with a locking mechanism",
        "C: A dispatch layer partitions deliveries across drivers before route optimization runs"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — a dispatch/partitioning layer that assigns deliveries to specific drivers before route optimization. This separates assignment (dispatch context) from sequencing (route planning context) and scales to multi-driver zones."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define what constitutes a 'manual deviation' from the route. Without a clear definition, the system may either miss real deviations or trigger constant false-positive recalculations from GPS noise.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Driver explicitly reports deviation via app action",
        "B: GPS-based detection with configurable distance threshold (e.g., >500m from next stop's approach path)",
        "C: Detected when driver completes a stop out of sequence"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C as primary (out-of-sequence completion) with Option B as supplementary. Sequence-based detection is deterministic and avoids GPS noise issues."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec does not define the vehicle class taxonomy or whether it is a strict hierarchy. A motorcycle-to-freight example is given, but the full classification (motorcycle, car, van, truck, freight) and whether higher classes subsume lower classes is undefined. This directly affects BR-6 implementation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers", "Operations Team", "Compliance/Legal"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define a strict linear hierarchy where each class can handle all classes below it",
        "B: Define a capability matrix where each vehicle class has explicit eligible delivery types"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — a capability matrix is more accurate for real-world logistics. A refrigerated van can carry perishables but a large truck cannot. Linear hierarchy oversimplifies."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Shift-end semantics are undefined. When a driver's shift ends with undelivered stops remaining on their route, the spec does not say what happens to those deliveries. They could be lost in limbo if not explicitly returned to the pending pool.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations Team", "Drivers"],
      "options": [
        "A: Automatically return all undelivered stops to 'pending' and trigger reassignment",
        "B: Transfer remaining stops to the next available driver in the zone",
        "C: Flag for manual operations team intervention"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A with notification to ops — undelivered stops automatically return to pending, and operations is alerted to reassign or escalate."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
